title: The Inherited Grove Where Deeds Dissolved

features.name: fence-posts-returning
features.desc-on-touch: boundary markers that have learned they are not the boundary; the land continues beyond the line you thought was final.

features.name: boundary-stone-garden
features.desc-on-touch: where property corners turned into living things; granite remembering it was once the edge of someone's claim.

features.name: deeds-as-leaves
features.desc-on-touch: documents of ownership falling; each leaf holds a name of someone who thought they owned the ground.

features.name: compass-rose-learning
features.desc-on-touch: a surveyor's instrument that never finished its work; the land moved while it was looking.

features.name: title-tree
features.desc-on-touch: you stand before the tree where the deed is buried; it has grown around the papers and learned to ignore the claim.

inscription: the forest kept better records than any deed ever did

file.name: SurveyorReport_EastProperty_Dissolution_1984.txt
file.note: the afternoon the boundary stones learned they were never the edge of anything

reverie.title: REVERIE — the inherited acre where letting go began
narr: you walk the line your grandmother drew on maps fifty
years ago, and the trees have forgotten it was ever straight.
she stops at a corner stone, running her hand across names
carved by surveyors long dead. 'it never belonged to anyone,
she says quietly. 'we were just the ones who forgot that.'

exits.label: the boundary stones growing into the distance
deeper into a forest that unmaps itself
toward older title trees where the first names still linger
back to the place where the deed was buried
